speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing our interview with Rose Kaplovitz, Rose K-A-P-L-O-W-V-I-T-Z, interviewer is Ruth Resnikoff. This is tape two. Rose, you began to tell me about how the Germans methodically began to take people away. Could you start that again? Was it 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the end of 1940. They-- first, even before that, they terrorized us in different ways, such as I remember one day-- do you mind if I go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course not.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day they came and asked my father for his identification card. And they went to each household and they asked for the IDs of each man of the home. And that they were going to hang three people and they wanted people from each home to witness it so as to instill fear in all of us. And I'll never forget that because that day we kids were playing in the street, and we found out that they are going to hang three men. And we thought, oh, let's go and see, as though this was some kind of a circus, or something. I have never seen a Western movie. I have never witnessed a hanging, so it was a novelty. It was something like exciting, something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all ran into the square also. And I'll never forget it as I was standing there and waiting, not knowing what I was about to see. It was a gorgeous, sunny day, and unconsciously, or subconsciously, I was humming to myself a song that Jeanette MacDonlad sang in the movie about San Francisco, San Francisco, this has Polish words to it. And I was humming that song to myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, a horrible scream went out from the whole crowd, and I looked up and saw the three men hanging. I cannot forget the sight because of the song. Whenever I hear that melody-- on the radio, on television, or wherever-- I see the three men hanging. So those are things that I can't forget because of a song. Others can probably forget because they viewed it. But it was, again, another means of instilling fear in us. But as I was saying before, that how we were being deceived all the time. You know, they would come suddenly at night, force your front door open, and by the time you were awakened, by the time you began to realize what's happening, several members of your home were taken away. Or from time to time, they would send a notification that you are to report to the Umschlagplatz, for young people. But it was during one of those raids, those nightly raids, that they surrounded our street and it was lit up and they forced their way into our home that one of my sisters was taken away. And she, my sister Mania, she was the first one to be sent to a labor camp, deep into Germany. At first we used to get postcards from her, telling us that she's working, that everything is orderly. We knew and realized that this is what she is forced to say, that she cannot really open up and say what she feels. It was painful because our family was growing smaller all the time. My sister who lived in Kielce, we lost contact with her completely. And my brother was there. Then here my sister was taken away. So our family was growing smaller all the time, and the sadness was setting in as time went on. I remember in '41 and '42, there were no more books to be gotten for a Jewish child because the libraries were closed to us. You could not-- in Poland, in order to be a member of a library, you had to be a member and pay dues. We were not allowed to be members anymore, so even books were not available to us. It was a very difficult childhood because of that. But somehow, life went on the best we could, and we still were filled with hope. We did not realize that time would get much worse. We thought--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was bad enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We thought that, after all, my father had so much faith. President Roosevelt of America will not let them do such things to us. We were always wondering, where's the world? Where is the whole civilization, that nobody-- that nobody speaks on our behalf, that no one does anything. We were always hoping that, how far can they go without the world awakening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any radio contact with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Radio was-- completely-- there were no more radios for us. That was taken away, too. We were forbidden to have a radio, as a matter of fact. And even when-- at one point-- I believe that was in '41, or might have been even in '40, yeah, 41-- that Hitler, a train with Hitler on it was passing through our city. All the Jews as well as the Poles were forbidden that we could leave our homes. They didn't want any of us on the street. Not that he disembarked or anything, but no one was allowed on the street that particular day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but somehow life went on, always hoping, always believing that this would end tomorrow, but it wasn't meant to be. In summer of 1942-- and how can I forget-- there was-- we were ordered to report to the Umschlagplatz, which was a gathering point. We were told it would be a routine check. We would have our IDs stamped, and we would be released. But somehow it was so important to have that ID. Without it, no food rations. Without food rations, how long would you survive? So it was out of necessity that we had to report.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being told that it was a routine check, we just went in the clothes we wear a wearing. But no sooner had we reported to the Umschlagplatz, we were soon surrounded by Gestapo and SS. And we were kept there for the next two days and two nights. The Umschlagplatz was a sports field that had so many pleasant memories for me, personally. It was the place where we used to go ice skate during the winter. It was the place where my brother used to play soccer. But as long as I shall live, I shall not forget the nightmare of the Umschlagplatz. There were close to 30,000 people gathered there, and we were kept there for two days, for two nights without food, without water. Can you imagine the old people who were sick, who collapsed. Once they collapsed, they were soon removed, never to be seen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the children, the infants whose cries never ceased? How does a mother explain to a two year old that there's no water to be gotten for him because he is a Jew? Could my children, could your children understand that at the age of two or three? The cries of the people-- How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hung onto my mother and my father and I cried, too. I cried because I was petrified. I didn't know of what I was petrified. I didn't know what was to happen, but I cried because I was-- first of all, at night it rained and we got wet, and it was cold. And here was my mother and my father who always met my needs, and they could do nothing for me. They were so helpless. And I couldn't understand why this was happening to me, to all of us. You know, at night it rained. We were cold. We cuddled, filled with fear, not knowing, not realizing what the next morning will bring. Finally, on the third morning, the great selection began. We were ordered to stand in long files, and in front of each file stood a Gestapo--

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of each?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each file of people, of each line, stood a Gestapo or an SS man. They would put a stick in front of them. That stick sort of served as a separation. I believe it was a mental separation as well as physical, that we were some kind of a germ and we could not approach them too close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I was approaching that Nazi dressed in black uniform, I was thinking, who is he? What is he doing? Though to me, he looked like a devil, on that day, that horrible day that I shall never forget, he and those standing there in front of us, played the role of gods. For each of these men, of these human beings, as we called them, was deciding that day who shall live and who shall die. And all it took is a turn of a thumb. As we were approaching them, the selection was swift. Right, left, right, left. You looked at them, and they didn't even see you. It seemed like what are they doing? We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those sent to the right were young, healthy, strong. Among them, another one of my sisters. Soon these young people were loaded on trucks and eventually they, too, were shipped to labor concentration camps. Some people were released, allowed to go home. Those sent to the left-- among them my father, my mother, my younger sister, and myself-- were slated to be sent to Auschwitz. Auschwitz is a small town located not very far from my hometown, yet it has become the place where the great German scientists, architects, engineers, doctors have developed the most effective, most efficient death factory. Everyone knows of Auschwitz, but in that period of time, in 1942, in the summer, we did not even know that such a place exists. And today I wonder about the Nazi guards. They knew where they are sending us. Because there were close to 8,000 people that we're going to be shipped to Auschwitz-- over 8,000-- they placed us in two buildings where we would have to wait our turn. And somehow, we were fortunate because we managed to escape. In that chaos, my mother and my younger sister were separated from my father and me and they ended up in two-- we ended up in two different buildings. But my father and I managed to escape with the help of a militia man-- that means a Jewish policeman who worked for the Judenrat. He was a boarder in our house, for when the Nazis entered we had to give a part of our home up and share it with other families. And since he knew us very well and since he lived in our home, he helped us escape. He took us in the middle of the night, my father and myself, to the roof of the building, and from there he told us, you will have to go through several buildings, but be careful, and you will make it safely home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You crossed from one roof to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to another, yes, and we made it safely home. We immediately tried to inquire of the whereabouts of my mother and my younger sister, and when we found in which building she was, she too was helped by our family physician, Dr. Plavner, who was a neighbor of ours, who helped them escape in garbage vans. So here our family was reunited by that time, again--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know more about that escape? How did they get into the garbage--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he got them into a vans that were being taken out of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was he Judenrat, too, this doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jew. He was a neighbor of ours. I'll never forget him because he also had a little girl my age who had a governess. That child was so pampered. She never played with us in the yard. She was always sitting on the balcony with her governess and envying us. Sometimes I would be called to go and play with her. I did, but I really didn't like to. I wanted to be with the kids. And when I think today how deprived that child was, I know that on the way to Auschwitz her father injected something in her and put her to sleep so she wouldn't wake up alive anymore. But it was he, a good neighbor of ours, who helped my mother and my younger sister. So we were reunited, but for a very short while, because soon after that there was an announcement that we were to take with us whatever we could carry. We would be placed in a ghetto. You see, a ghetto was a necessary thing because it brought us all closer together. It gave the Nazis a better control of us, and, and eased in deportation-- eventual deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does one decide what to take? There were no moving vans. There were no cars, no buggies, no horses. How do you decide what to take from your home, only what you can carry in your hands. How much can you carry in your hands? What do you take? Your clothing, your bedding, pictures, utensils. It was a very difficult decision, and can you imagine what a ghetto is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I was born in this home. All my life I lived on the same street, and I had to leave the neighborhood that I knew, that I loved, and go to the edge of town where the ghetto was located. In our case, it was on the outskirts of our hometown. There was a railroad track that led right up to the ghetto gate, and we were placed one family per room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So try to imagine having to share a home such as we have today with as many families as there are rooms. Total strangers from different walks of life, and you have to learn to get along with these people. We were assigned to the kitchen, and through the kitchen everybody had to walk through. It was difficult time. But what was a ghetto? Each ghetto, of course, was either surrounded by brick wall or barbed wire. There were guards all around the ghetto, all the time. And what is there in the ghetto? Of course, there are no synagogues to go and pray. There are no schools. There are no libraries. There are no bicycles. There are no toys. There are no books. There is no paper. There is no pencil. There is nothing. It seems in the ghetto just emptiness, hunger, starvation, epidemics, and death all around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have such horrible memories of life in the ghetto. Suddenly it seemed like my life has collapsed and I was just thrown into a pit from which I could not possibly escape. It was a terribly difficult time in my young life, at that time. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me some details about the food rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the food rations were still-- my father would get the food rations for our family. It was not very much to eat, but somehow, as long as we were together, that was the main thing-- to hold, to be together. There were families who, during the constant raids, which they never ceased-- during the constant raids, families were broken up. You see, there was a definite program, definite plan. The young, the able-bodied young, strong people were being shipped to labor camps where they would help the Nazis in their war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: The old people or the very young-- by old I mean anyone over 35-- or young kids, like myself-- kids under 15 or so-- were rendered useless by the Nazis. Therefore, the strong and young people were being constantly taken away, shipped to labor camps, the others to Auschwitz, unknown to us. We were constantly told that we would be resettled to a different city since ours was overcrowded. And I don't know if we chose to believe that, or if we believed it because who could imagine that there is such a place as Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what it did, it broke up the family unit. And by breaking up the family unit, we each served as a hostage. We knew that if we get out of line, my sisters could suffer, be killed for it, and they, in turn, felt the same thing. So when today you are asked, why did you not fight back or-- although, there was, in every ghetto, in every concentration camp, there was an uprising of a sort, or sabotage there of a sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- such as like in our-- in our ghetto, there was formed organization, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]-- it was two brothers-- they started organizing an underground movement. But the two leaders were caught, shot, and they broke it up. But even if they wouldn't be caught, what were the weapons that we had to fight? Without the outside help of the Polish underground, it was impossible to rise against the Nazis. Today, if you hear of wars or uprisings, you know that these people get weapons from somebody. Nobody in this world, this whole huge world, nobody helped us by supplying us with weapons. Nobody helped us by supplying us with the barest necessities, such as food, to keep our souls intact, to keep our mind working. And also, you must take into consideration that if you are 18, or 17, or 19, and you are ready to die for a cause, you must take into consideration that if you should be caught you definitely will die, and you are prepared for it. But you also must be able to bear the responsibility that if you get caught, your whole family will also be wiped out, and sometimes a whole village. Which was the case in [NON-ENGLISH]. One, one young man killed one German soldier, and the whole village was wiped out. So it wasn't easy just to go and fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, most of the people fought with bare hands. How can you fight an enemy composed of barbarians, allowed to go unchallenged by a world, by a civilization that chooses to look the other way? That's the painful thing about it. But what about in the ghetto? There were children in the ghetto like myself, children whose parents were taken away during the previous raids. There was an orphanage in the ghetto, but the children became streetwise because life made them so. They realized that whenever there was a raid, the orphanage was to first place raided, because again, the young people were rendered useless, so they were the first ones to be shipped to Auschwitz. And a lot of those kids refused to go to the orphanage. Where did they live? In attics, in basements, in the field. They ate whatever they could find, steal or, as they called it, organize.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a boy who was my best girlfriend's brother. His whole family was taken away, and he was the only one that remained. My mother ask him if he would like to come and stay with us, but he chose not to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he saw that there was no room. We had only one bed in that room that was for my mother, my father, my younger sister, and myself. And he got used to, already, living in the street, I guess with other boys and girls. And I think maybe he felt more comfortable there than in a family unit. Of course, he, like everybody else, perished, too. I-- because of-- you know, because of the constant raids of the Nazi raids, people began building bunkers. You know, no matter what, you always want to make it one more day, always hoping that tomorrow it will end. In our case, we built also a bunker. It was in the basement of the building. It was a windowless wall. And parallel to that we built another wall from old bricks. You had to descend to the bunker from the floor above, but always somebody with a green card. Green card, that was like, that meant that you were employed, and that your employment was necessary. These people were, for the time, being safe, so to speak. If you had to hide in the bunker, somebody like that would have to be left behind and block, or some put-- push something over the entrance so they wouldn't discover that you are there. It was helpful maybe a little bit from the beginning, but later as they came with dogs, and there was no way of escaping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have this green card?

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, yes he did. But you know, those raids, in order to-- you know, from beginning they would come loud, and you would hear the boots. But soon the Nazis learned to creep up-- up on you like a cat, quietly. And in order to be able to make it to the bunker, many nights people would have to sit on the roof and keep guard and warn the people inside the building that there is a-- a-- there is a raid approaching. And whenever my father had to sit on the roof, I would go with him. And as horrible as those nights might have been, they hold such pleasant memories in my heart for me alone because, you know, we were seven children. And my father really never had the time for me alone. And here on the roof, I had my father all to myself, just for me. And it was here on the roof that my father would be telling me about his childhood, about my grandparents whom I did not remember much. From time to time he would practice with me multiplication and division, as though to give me courage that I still need to know those things, that there is still hope, that there is still a future for me. Or from time to time, he would make me recite Polish poetry, which was also a vital part of school. I shall never, never forget those nights because this was the first time I had my father all to myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember those nights because I want, because I choose to remember, because this was really a time for me and my father alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop and we'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
